methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Brilliance, a Series B quantum computing company led by founder Mark Luo, is evaluated against a comp universe of 11 peers: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private firms including Q-CTRL, Quantinuum, and others in the sector.

The stage-progression method applies here and anchors the valuation. Using the company's $50M total raised as a baseline value proxy and applying a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical for Series B progression yields $95M. The peer-median method is not applicable; while 7 same-sector peers exist, only 1 occupies the same stage, with 0 in adjacent stages and 0 in similar valuation tiers—insufficient for a credible median benchmark.

The triangulated analysis, constrained to a single applicable method, supports a valuation range of $81M (low) to $109M (high), with a median of $95M. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrowly comparable Series B quantum computing companies in the dataset, which limits cross-validation of the stage-progression multiple. The stage-progression multiplier itself carries execution risk typical of the stage, and quantum computing's rapid technical differentiation means peer multiples may not fully reflect Quantum Brilliance's specific technological positioning. Additional detail on burn rate, customer traction, and IP portfolio would strengthen conviction in this range.
